hero.tagline: South Carolina's Magistrate Courts handle claims up to $7,500 with a streamlined process designed for self-representation.

at_a_glance.label: Most you can sue for
at_a_glance.detail: Same cap for individuals and businesses

at_a_glance.label: Recent change
at_a_glance.detail: No major changes in the last 3 years

limits.split_claims_explanation: If your claim exceeds $7,500, you cannot split it into multiple cases to fit within the limit. You must either waive the excess amount or file in a higher court.

claim_categories.title: Contracts and money owed
claim_categories.blurb: South Carolina allows you 3 years to file claims related to contracts and debts, whether written or oral.

claims.name: Breach of a written contract
claims.example: A contractor signed a $5,000 contract to remodel your kitchen, took the deposit, and never completed the work.
claims.notes: You have 3 years from the date of breach to file. Bring the signed contract, payment records, and any correspondence.

claims.name: Breach of an oral contract
claims.example: You agreed verbally to lend a friend $2,000, which they promised to repay in six months but haven't.
claims.notes: The statute of limitations is 3 years from the breach. Gather any evidence of the agreement, such as emails or witness statements.

claims.name: Unpaid personal loan
claims.example: You loaned $3,000 to a colleague who agreed to repay in installments but stopped after the first payment.
claims.notes: File within 3 years from the date the loan was due. Provide documentation of the loan and any payments made.

claims.name: Default on a promissory note
claims.example: A borrower signed a promissory note for $6,000 but failed to make any payments.
claims.notes: You have 3 years from the date of default to file. Present the original promissory note and records of non-payment.

claims.name: Unpaid invoice for services rendered
claims.example: You provided consulting services worth $4,500, invoiced the client, but haven't received payment.
claims.notes: The statute of limitations is 3 years from the invoice date. Bring the invoice, service agreement, and any communication regarding payment.

claims.name: Unpaid balance on an open account
claims.example: A customer has an outstanding balance of $2,000 on their account with your business.
claims.notes: File within 3 years from the last payment or charge. Provide account statements and records of transactions.

claims.name: Unpaid rent by tenant
claims.example: Your tenant owes $1,500 in unpaid rent for the past two months.
claims.notes: You have 3 years to file from the date rent was due. Bring the lease agreement and records of missed payments.

claims.name: Payment with a bad check
claims.example: A customer paid for goods worth $500 with a check that was returned for insufficient funds.
claims.notes: After providing a 30-day written notice, you can file if the check isn't made good. Include the bounced check and notice documentation.
claims.damage_boost: Check amount + service charge up to $30 + civil penalty up to $500 OR 2x check amount, whichever is greater.

claims.name: Co-signer seeking repayment
claims.example: You co-signed a $5,000 loan for a friend who defaulted, and you had to pay the lender.
claims.notes: File within 3 years from the date you made payment. Provide the loan agreement and proof of your payments.

claim_categories.title: Consumer disputes
claim_categories.blurb: South Carolina provides a 3-year statute of limitations for most consumer-related claims, allowing you to seek redress for defective products or services.

claims.name: Purchase of a defective product
claims.example: You bought a $1,200 appliance that stopped working within a month, and the seller refuses to repair or replace it.
claims.notes: You have 3 years from the date of purchase to file. Bring the purchase receipt, warranty, and any communication with the seller.

claims.name: Substandard service provided
claims.example: A cleaning service charged you $300 but did not perform the agreed-upon tasks.
claims.notes: File within 3 years from the service date. Provide the service agreement, receipts, and evidence of the poor service.

claims.name: Faulty auto repair
claims.example: A mechanic charged $2,000 for repairs that didn't fix the issue, and the car remains inoperable.
claims.notes: You have 3 years to file from the repair date. Bring repair invoices, work orders, and any expert evaluations.

claims.name: Unsatisfactory work by a contractor
claims.example: A contractor was paid $6,500 to renovate your bathroom but left the job incomplete and substandard.
claims.notes: File within 3 years from the date of breach. Provide the contract, payment records, and photos of the incomplete work.

claims.name: Vendor failed to deliver services
claims.example: You paid a DJ $800 for an event, but they didn't show up, and you couldn't find a replacement.
claims.notes: You have 3 years to file from the event date. Bring the service agreement, payment proof, and any communication.

claims.name: Unauthorized charges after subscription cancellation
claims.example: You canceled a $50/month gym membership, but they continued to charge your account for three months.
claims.notes: File within 3 years from the first unauthorized charge. Provide cancellation confirmation and bank statements.

claims.name: Purchase of a defective used car
claims.example: You bought a used car for $7,000 that broke down within a week, and the seller refuses to refund or repair.
claims.notes: You have 3 years from the purchase date to file. Bring the bill of sale, any warranties, and repair estimates.

claims.name: Misleading advertising leading to financial loss
claims.example: A company advertised a product for $500 with features it didn't have, leading you to purchase under false pretenses.
claims.notes: File within 3 years from the date of purchase. Provide the advertisement, purchase receipt, and any correspondence.
claims.damage_boost: Potential for treble damages and attorney's fees under the Unfair Trade Practices Act.

claim_categories.title: Property damage
claim_categories.blurb: For property damage claims, South Carolina allows a 3-year statute of limitations, enabling you to seek compensation for damages to your property.

claims.name: Damage from a vehicle collision
claims.example: Another driver ran a red light and caused $4,000 in damage to your car.
claims.notes: You have 3 years from the accident date to file. Bring the police report, repair estimates, and photos of the damage.

claims.name: Neighbor's actions damaged your property
claims.example: A neighbor's tree fell onto your fence, causing $1,500 in damage.
claims.notes: File within 3 years from the date of damage. Provide photos, repair estimates, and any communication with the neighbor.

claims.name: Tenant caused damage to rental property
claims.example: A tenant left your rental unit with $2,000 in damages beyond normal wear and tear.
claims.notes: You have 3 years from the end of tenancy to file. Bring the lease agreement, photos, and repair invoices.

claims.name: Damage caused by someone else's pet
claims.example: A neighbor's dog entered your yard and destroyed $800 worth of landscaping.
claims.notes: File within 3 years from the incident. Provide photos, repair estimates, and any witness statements.

claims.name: Damage to property left in someone's care
claims.example: You left a $1,200 watch with a jeweler for repair, and they returned it with scratches.
claims.notes: You have 3 years from the date of damage to file. Bring the service agreement and photos of the damage.

claim_categories.title: Landlord-tenant disputes
claim_categories.blurb: South Carolina provides a 3-year statute of limitations for monetary disputes between landlords and tenants, excluding eviction proceedings.

claims.name: Failure to return security deposit
claims.example: After moving out, your landlord failed to return your $1,000 security deposit without explanation.
claims.notes: Landlords must return the deposit within 30 days. File within 3 years if they fail to do so. Bring the lease agreement and move-out documentation.

claims.name: Failure to provide itemized deductions from deposit
claims.example: Your landlord deducted $500 from your deposit without providing an itemized list of damages.
claims.notes: Landlords must provide an itemized list within 30 days. File within 3 years if they fail to do so. Bring the lease and any correspondence.

claims.name: Rental unit deemed uninhabitable
claims.example: Your apartment had severe mold issues, and the landlord refused to address them, forcing you to move out.
claims.notes: You have 3 years to file from the date you vacated. Provide photos, health reports, and communication with the landlord.

claims.name: Landlord wrongfully retained tenant's property
claims.example: After eviction, your landlord kept your furniture valued at $2,500.
claims.notes: File within 3 years from the date of retention. Bring the lease, inventory of items, and any communication.

claims.name: Landlord conducted an illegal eviction
claims.example: Your landlord changed the locks without notice, preventing you from accessing your apartment.
claims.notes: You have 3 years to file from the eviction date. Provide the lease, photos, and any witness statements.

claims.name: Failure to return prepaid rent after lease termination
claims.example: You paid first and last month's rent, but after early termination, the landlord didn't refund the unused portion.
claims.notes: File within 3 years from lease termination. Bring the lease agreement and payment records.

claims.name: Tenant failed to pay rent
claims.example: Your tenant missed three months of rent totaling $3,000 before vacating.
claims.notes: You have 3 years from the last missed payment to file. Provide the lease and payment history.

claims.name: Tenant repaired and deducted from rent
claims.example: You fixed a broken heater for $600 after the landlord's inaction and deducted it from rent; the landlord is now suing for unpaid rent.
claims.notes: Ensure you followed proper notice procedures before repair. File within 3 years if the landlord disputes. Bring repair receipts and notices.

claim_categories.title: Employment and wage disputes
claim_categories.blurb: South Carolina allows a 3-year statute of limitations for wage-related claims, enabling employees to recover unpaid wages or benefits.

claims.name: Employer failed to pay earned wages
claims.example: Your employer owes you $2,000 for hours worked but hasn't issued payment.
claims.notes: File within 3 years from the date wages were due. Provide timesheets and any communication regarding payment.

claims.name: Employer withheld final paycheck
claims.example: After resigning, your employer refused to pay your last paycheck of $1,500.
claims.notes: You have 3 years to file from the date the final paycheck was due. Bring resignation notice and payroll records.

claims.name: Employer failed to pay earned commissions
claims.example: You earned $3,000 in sales commissions that your employer hasn't paid.
claims.notes: File within 3 years from the date commissions were due. Provide sales records and commission agreements.

claims.name: Employer didn't pay overtime wages
claims.example: You worked 10 hours of overtime but weren't compensated at the overtime rate, totaling $500.
claims.notes: You have 3 years to file from the date overtime was worked. Bring timesheets and payroll records.

claim_categories.title: Personal claims
claim_categories.blurb: South Carolina provides a 3-year statute of limitations for personal injury and property-related claims, allowing individuals to seek compensation for damages.

claims.name: Minor personal injury
claims.example: You slipped and fell in a store, resulting in a sprained ankle and $1,200 in medical bills.
claims.notes: File within 3 years from the injury date. Provide medical records and incident reports.

claims.name: Damage to loaned property
claims.example: You lent a friend your $800 camera, which they returned with a broken lens.
claims.notes: You have 3 years from the date of damage to file. Bring evidence of the loan and photos of the damage.

claims.name: Theft of personal property
claims.example: A neighbor took your $1,500 bicycle without permission and refuses to return it.
claims.notes: File within 3 years from the date of theft. Provide proof of ownership and any communication with the neighbor.

claim_categories.title: Vehicle-specific claims
claim_categories.blurb: South Carolina allows a 3-year statute of limitations for vehicle-related disputes, enabling individuals to seek compensation for damages or wrongful actions.

claims.name: Dispute over mechanic's repair work
claims.example: A mechanic charged $2,500 for repairs that didn't fix the issue, and the car remains inoperable.
claims.notes: You have 3 years to file from the repair date. Bring repair invoices, work orders, and any expert evaluations.

claims.name: Vehicle was wrongfully towed
claims.example: Your car was towed from a legal parking spot, and you had to pay $300 to retrieve it.
claims.notes: File within 3 years from the towing date. Provide photos of the parking spot and towing receipts.

exclusions.explanation: Magistrate Courts handle eviction proceedings, but they follow special timelines and procedures distinct from small claims cases.
Magistrate Courts do not have jurisdiction over cases where the title to real property is in question.
Defamation cases often involve complex legal issues and damages exceeding the small claims limit.
Professional malpractice claims typically exceed the small claims jurisdictional limit and require expert testimony.
Matters such as divorce, child custody, and probate are outside the jurisdiction of Magistrate Courts.
Claims involving federal law, such as bankruptcy or patents, are not handled in state small claims courts.
Class action lawsuits are complex and exceed the procedural scope of small claims courts.
Small claims courts cannot issue injunctions or orders for specific performance; they can only award monetary damages.
Suing the federal government involves specific procedures and is not within the jurisdiction of state small claims courts.
Workers' compensation claims are handled by the Workers' Compensation Commission, not small claims courts.
Disputes subject to mandatory arbitration clauses are typically resolved through arbitration, not small claims court.
Claims against deceased individuals must be filed against their estate in probate court.

damages.punitive_explanation: Punitive damages are not awarded in small claims court; however, statutory penalties may apply in certain cases, such as bad check claims.

statutory_multipliers.multiplier: 2x check amount or $500, whichever is greater
statutory_multipliers.explanation: Check amount + service charge up to $30 + civil penalty up to $500 OR 2x check amount, whichever is greater.

damages.attorney_fees_explanation: Attorney's fees may be recoverable in cases involving statutory violations, such as under the Unfair Trade Practices Act.

filing_options: Any county where one defendant resides
Where the cause of action arose

where_to_file.consequences_of_wrong_venue: Filing in the wrong venue may result in dismissal or transfer of the case, potentially causing delays and additional costs.
where_to_file.e_filing_notes: E-filing availability varies by county; check with the local Magistrate Court for specific procedures.

forms.name: Motion and Affidavit to Proceed In Forma Pauperis

eligibility_bullets: Receiving need-based public assistance (SNAP, TANF, SSI)
Household income below 125% federal poverty line
waiver.coverage_notes: The waiver covers filing fees and may cover service fees; check with the court for specifics.
waiver.if_you_win_notes: If you win, the waived fees may be added to the judgment against the loser.

fees.fees_recoverable_from_loser_notes: If you win, filing fees and reasonable service costs are added to the judgment.

entries.clock_starts: Date of last payment or charge

entries.notes: Discovery rule applies; clock starts when fraud is discovered or should have been discovered.

entries.clock_starts: Date deposit was due to be returned

statute_of_limitations.discovery_rule_notes: The discovery rule applies to fraud claims; the statute of limitations begins when the fraud is discovered or should have been discovered.
statute_of_limitations.tolling_notes: The statute of limitations may be tolled if the defendant is out of state, the plaintiff is a minor, or other specific circumstances apply.

pre_filing.demand_letter_notes: While not required, sending a demand letter can often resolve disputes without court intervention and serves as evidence of your attempt to settle.
pre_filing.bad_check_notice_rule: For bad check claims, you must send a 30-day written notice demanding payment before filing suit.
pre_filing.wage_claim_demand_rule: No specific pre-filing demand required for wage claims, but it's advisable to request payment in writing before filing.
pre_filing.landlord_tenant_pre_filing_notes: Landlords must return security deposits within 30 days; tenants should provide a forwarding address to facilitate this.
pre_filing.consumer_protection_notes: Under the Unfair Trade Practices Act, a demand letter is not required but can strengthen your case.

forms.name: Complaint
forms.description: Initiates a small claims case by outlining the plaintiff's allegations and desired relief.

forms.name: Summons
forms.description: Notifies the defendant of the lawsuit and provides instructions for responding.

forms.name: Answer
forms.description: Allows the defendant to respond to the allegations in the complaint.

forms.name: Counterclaim
forms.description: Enables the defendant to file a claim against the plaintiff within the same case.

forms.name: Motion and Affidavit to Proceed In Forma Pauperis
forms.description: Requests a waiver of court fees due to financial hardship.

forms.name: Notice of Appeal
forms.description: Notifies the court and opposing party of the intent to appeal a judgment.

methods.name: Sheriff personal service
methods.description: The sheriff or constable personally delivers the documents to the defendant.
cons: Potential delays depending on sheriff's workload

methods.name: Private process server
methods.description: A hired individual delivers the documents to the defendant.

methods.name: Certified mail
methods.description: Documents are sent via certified mail with return receipt requested.

service.business_service_rule: Serve the registered agent; if unavailable, serve an officer or managing agent.
service.out_of_state_defendant_notes: Service must comply with South Carolina rules and the laws of the state where the defendant resides.
service.cant_find_defendant_notes: If the defendant cannot be located, service by publication may be permitted with court approval.
service.avoiding_service_notes: If the defendant is evading service, document attempts and seek court permission for alternative methods.

response_and_default.response_notes: Defendants are not required to file a written answer but must appear at the scheduled hearing to avoid default judgment.
response_and_default.default_process: If the defendant fails to appear, the court may grant a default judgment in favor of the plaintiff.
response_and_default.prove_up_notes: Even if the defendant defaults, the plaintiff must present evidence to substantiate the claim before a judgment is entered.

motion_to_vacate_form.name: Motion to Set Aside Default Judgment

response_and_default.motion_to_vacate_appeal_notes: A motion to vacate must be filed within 10 days; if denied, an appeal must be filed within 30 days of the judgment.

counterclaim.transfer_to_higher_court_notes: If the counterclaim exceeds the jurisdictional limit, the case may be dismissed or the counterclaim reduced to fit within the limit.

hearing.lawyers_allowed_notes: Parties may be represented by attorneys but are not required to do so.
hearing.format: Bench trial with informal procedures; each party presents their case, and the judge may ask questions.
what_to_bring: Names and contact info for witnesses
hearing.interpreter_request_process: Parties must arrange for their own interpreters; some courts may provide a list of qualified interpreters.
hearing.decision_timing: On the spot or mailed within 10 days
hearing.on_site_mediation_notes: Some counties offer mediation services; check with the local court for availability.

appeals.type_notes: The appeal is a new trial in the Circuit Court, where both parties can present their cases anew.
appeals.bond_amount_notes: The appellant must post a bond to cover court costs and the judgment amount.
appeals.default_judgment_notes: A default judgment can be appealed, but it's advisable to first file a motion to vacate in the Magistrate Court.

collection.post_judgment_interest_notes: Interest accrues on the judgment amount until paid in full.

methods.name: Wage garnishment
methods.blurb: Obtain a portion of the debtor's wages until the judgment is satisfied.
methods.description: File a writ of garnishment with the court, serve the employer, and the employer withholds a portion of the debtor's wages to be applied toward the judgment.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if the debtor is employed and earns above the exemption threshold.
exemptions: 75% of disposable earnings or 30 times the federal minimum wage, whichever is greater

methods.name: Bank levy
methods.blurb: Seize funds from the debtor's bank account to satisfy the judgment.
methods.description: Obtain a writ of execution from the court, serve it on the debtor's bank, and the bank freezes and turns over funds to satisfy the judgment.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if the debtor has sufficient funds in the account; may be limited by exemptions.
exemptions: $5,000 in aggregate value of personal property, including cash and bank accounts

methods.name: Property lien
methods.blurb: Place a lien on the debtor's real property to secure the judgment.
methods.description: File the judgment with the county recorder's office to create a lien on the debtor's real property, which must be satisfied upon sale or refinancing.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if the debtor owns real property; may take time to realize payment.

methods.name: Writ of execution
methods.blurb: Seize and sell the debtor's non-exempt property to satisfy the judgment.
methods.description: Obtain a writ of execution from the court, have the sheriff seize non-exempt property, and sell it at public auction to satisfy the judgment.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if the debtor owns valuable non-exempt property; process can be time-consuming.
exemptions: Various personal property exemptions totaling up to $5,000

collection.judgment_renewal_process: File a motion to renew the judgment before the 10-year period expires; renewal extends the judgment for another 10 years.
collection.bankruptcy_effect_notes: Filing for bankruptcy imposes an automatic stay on collection efforts; certain judgments may be discharged in bankruptcy.
collection.priority_notes: Judgment liens are prioritized by the date of recording; earlier liens have higher priority.

businesses.self_representation_notes: Businesses can represent themselves in Magistrate Court without an attorney.
businesses.fictitious_name_notes: Businesses operating under a trade name must file a Certificate of Assumed Name with the county clerk.
businesses.sole_proprietor_employee_exception: Sole proprietors may authorize an employee to represent them in court.
businesses.licensing_notes: Unlicensed contractors may be barred from suing to recover fees for work requiring a license.

county_variations.detail: Charleston County charges a filing fee of $80 for small claims cases.
Richland County charges a filing fee of $90 for small claims cases.

pitfalls.title: Incorrect defendant information
pitfalls.what_happens: Suing the wrong party or using incorrect names can result in dismissal or unenforceable judgments.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Verify the exact legal name and address of the defendant before filing; for businesses, check with the Secretary of State.

pitfalls.title: Missing statute of limitations
pitfalls.what_happens: Filing after the statute of limitations expires leads to automatic dismissal of the case.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Determine the applicable statute of limitations for your claim type and file within that period.

pitfalls.title: Improper service of process
pitfalls.what_happens: Failure to serve the defendant correctly can delay the case or result in dismissal.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Follow the court's rules for service of process precisely, using approved methods and documenting service.

pitfalls.title: Overlooking counterclaims
pitfalls.what_happens: Defendants may file counterclaims, potentially turning the tables and resulting in a judgment against you.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Be prepared for possible counterclaims by reviewing the entire dispute and considering the defendant's perspective.

pitfalls.title: Ignoring court deadlines
pitfalls.what_happens: Missing filing deadlines or court dates can lead to dismissal or default judgments.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Keep a detailed calendar of all deadlines and court dates, and set reminders to ensure timely compliance.

pitfalls.title: Insufficient evidence
pitfalls.what_happens: Lack of adequate evidence can result in losing the case, even if your claim is valid.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Gather all relevant documents, photos, and witness statements to substantiate your claim before filing.

recent_changes.title: Increase in jurisdictional limit
recent_changes.description: The jurisdictional limit for small claims cases in South Carolina Magistrate Courts increased from $5,000 to $7,500, allowing more cases to be heard in this forum.

faqs.question: Do I need a lawyer?
faqs.answer: No, you are not required to have a lawyer in South Carolina Magistrate Court. The process is designed for individuals to represent themselves, though you may choose to hire an attorney if you prefer.

faqs.question: How long does it take?
faqs.answer: The timeline varies, but most small claims cases in South Carolina are resolved within 30 to 90 days from filing to judgment.

faqs.question: What's the maximum I can sue for?
faqs.answer: In South Carolina Magistrate Court, you can sue for up to $7,500. If your claim exceeds this amount, you must file in a higher court or waive the excess.

faqs.question: What happens if the defendant doesn't show up?
faqs.answer: If the defendant fails to appear at the scheduled hearing, the court may grant a default judgment in your favor, provided you present sufficient evidence to support your claim.

faqs.question: Can I appeal?
faqs.answer: Yes, either party can appeal a Magistrate Court decision to the Circuit Court within 30 days of the judgment. The appeal is a trial de novo, meaning the case is heard anew.

faqs.question: How long is a judgment good for?
faqs.answer: A judgment in South Carolina is valid for 10 years from the date it is entered. You can renew the judgment before it expires to extend its enforceability.

state_specific_notes: South Carolina's Magistrate Courts handle both small claims and landlord-tenant disputes, including evictions.
The state allows for service of process via certified mail with return receipt requested, providing a cost-effective option.
Magistrate Court judgments can be appealed to the Circuit Court within 30 days, where the case is heard anew.
South Carolina law permits wage garnishment to satisfy judgments, subject to certain exemptions.
The state has a homestead exemption of $50,000, protecting a portion of a debtor's primary residence from creditors.

interesting_findings: South Carolina's Magistrate Courts have jurisdiction over both civil and criminal matters, providing a broad scope of authority.
The state offers a standardized set of forms for small claims proceedings, simplifying the filing process for litigants.
South Carolina's Unfair Trade Practices Act allows for treble damages and attorney's fees in certain consumer protection cases.
The state's small claims procedures are designed to be accessible to self-represented litigants, with simplified rules and informal hearings.
South Carolina permits service of process by publication when defendants cannot be located, ensuring cases can proceed.

sources.label: S.C. Code Title 22 Chapter 3 (Magistrate civil jurisdiction)
S.C. Code § 39-5-10 et seq. (Unfair Trade Practices)
